caveats: a: The BLS industry chart covers own-brand and contract factories together.
a: O*NET task descriptions are national occupation profiles, not observations of eligible plants.
a: Robotics may automate physical tasks but is outside pure AI substitution unless coupled to implementable capital projects.
rho: No current adoption survey isolates US contract footwear factories.
rho: Implementation economics vary between molded, cemented, stitched, and handcrafted footwear.
rho: High style turnover can erode training-data reuse.

e: Some firms combine brands, retail, and contract production.
e: The frozen firm-count estimate is itself margin-bridged and may misclassify band membership.
s5: Intentions differ from completed control transfers.
s5: Gallup spans all employer industries and sizes.
s5: A strategic buyer may acquire a brand but close or offshore its factory, failing the transferable-operation test.

q: Government cost or pricing clauses may differ from commercial private-label agreements.
q: Retention can be higher for scarce specialty capacity and lower for portable commodity styles.
d5: The 2019-2024 interval includes pandemic disruption.
d5: Import customs values and domestic sectoral output are not directly comparable measures.
d5: Tariffs, Berry Amendment procurement, and supply-chain policy can materially alter domestic demand.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B production runs priced per pair, style, batch, or contract, often with development, sampling, material procurement, quality control, and packaging bundled into the manufacturing relationship.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS code groups factories by product and does not distinguish own-brand production from outsourced manufacturing. The lens is narrowed to contract, OEM, and private-label factories because only those firms provide the recurring external service required by the fixed screen.
lens.excluded_activities: Brand-owned factories producing only proprietary footwear, footwear brands that outsource all production, wholesalers and retailers, repair shops, orthopedic extension footwear, captive internal units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent US footwear factories that repeatedly manufacture footwear for external brands, retailers, distributors, or government customers under contract, OEM, private-label, or white-label arrangements.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment counts for the whole footwear-manufacturing industry and O*NET describes occupation tasks, neither measures wage-weighted AI substitution in LMM contract factories. I map low exposure to hands-on machine operation, alignment, assembly, maintenance, and material handling; moderate exposure to inspection support, work-order interpretation, pattern/layout assistance, planning, quoting, documentation, and office work. This may overstate exposure if eligible small-batch factories are more craft-intensive, or understate it if computer vision reliably automates inspection.
caveats: The BLS industry chart covers own-brand and contract factories together.
O*NET task descriptions are national occupation profiles, not observations of eligible plants.
Robotics may automate physical tasks but is outside pure AI substitution unless coupled to implementable capital projects.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task and time studies from 8-12 eligible factories, including pattern engineering, sampling, quoting, purchasing, production planning, cutting, stitching, lasting, inspection, packing, compliance, and customer-service work, tested against available AI and vision systems.
a.rationale: BLS reports that the largest May 2024 occupations were 3,180 shoe-machine operators and 2,580 shoe and leather workers, far ahead of managers and office roles. O*NET shows the machine occupation centers on physically aligning parts, operating machinery, loading thread and materials, inspecting output, and maintaining equipment. AI can assist specifications, scheduling, design translation, costing, quality triage, and administration, but most current labor is embodied in production.

d5.bridge: BLS real sectoral output measures all NAICS 316210 production, not future demand for the eligible contract subset. The index fell from 99.028 in 2019 to 75.404 in 2024, including a pandemic drop, and USITC reports $27.319 billion of footwear imports in 2024 versus a much smaller domestic production base. I temper straight-line extrapolation because 2020 disrupted factories and domestic contract capacity may benefit from short lead times, procurement rules, or reshoring. The whole-industry trend may understate niche contract demand but import competition may also cap it.
caveats: The 2019-2024 interval includes pandemic disruption.
Import customs values and domestic sectoral output are not directly comparable measures.
Tariffs, Berry Amendment procurement, and supply-chain policy can materially alter domestic demand.
d5.change_evidence: Eligible-factory unit order books by customer/end market, domestic procurement forecasts, announced reshoring awards, USITC import quantities, and BLS real output through 2026, separated between contract and own-brand production.
d5.rationale: The historic real-output trend and overwhelming import base point to continued pressure on the current domestic service basket. The remaining market can be durable in military, work/safety, molded, specialty, fast-turn, and Made-in-USA niches, producing a central case of moderate real contraction rather than disappearance.

caveats: Public marketing may advertise contract capability even when most revenue is proprietary product.
Some firms combine brands, retail, and contract production.
The frozen firm-count estimate is itself margin-bridged and may misclassify band membership.
e.change_evidence: Classify all 52 frozen-band firms using websites, customer references, government awards, labels, interviews, and revenue records, and require a material recurring share of sales from external-brand or private-label production.
e.rationale: The Census definition identifies the product made but not whether the plant manufactures for its own brand or an external customer. Current US examples explicitly offer contract and private-label production, demonstrating that the qualifying model exists, but the domestic industry also contains vertically integrated branded makers. Roughly one-third of frozen-band firms is used as the central qualifying share pending firm-level classification.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $540.3M / sectoral output (T30) $1,650.8M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $196,725 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 316210); class <5: 99 firms, avg revenue/firm $449,556 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $68,557 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,500,968 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $228,898 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,106,722 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $626,275 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,077,610 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,079,335 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $45,411,364 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,925,233 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $71,625,071 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,922,823 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 52

caveats: Demand may migrate to larger foreign operators even if an operator remains necessary globally.
This primitive concerns operator necessity, not employment intensity.
Highly automated molded footwear may require fewer people but still requires a responsible plant operator.
o.change_evidence: Customer sourcing interviews on insourcing and factoryless production plans plus evidence of commercially deployed end-to-end autonomous footwear lines that can switch styles and remain accountable for quality and compliance.
o.rationale: Nearly all surviving outsourced footwear quantity still requires an accountable factory to source and handle materials, run cutting/assembly/lasting/finishing equipment, assure fit and quality, and deliver compliant goods. AI and software can reduce coordination and inspection labor but do not provide the physical production service; only customer insourcing or fully autonomous lines remove the operator.

caveats: No public dataset measures contract repricing or savings sharing.
Government cost or pricing clauses may differ from commercial private-label agreements.
Retention can be higher for scarce specialty capacity and lower for portable commodity styles.
q.change_evidence: Factory contracts and five years of bid, renewal, gross-margin, customer-concentration, cost-pass-through, and win/loss data segmented by private label, government, work/safety, and consumer-brand customers.
q.rationale: Per-pair and per-run manufacturing prices allow an operator to retain productivity gains between bids. However, sophisticated brands and government buyers can benchmark conversion costs, repeat orders are repriced, and imported footwear creates an exceptionally strong price ceiling. Specialty, short lead time, Made-in-USA, small-batch, and regulated-contract capabilities support some retention.

caveats: No current adoption survey isolates US contract footwear factories.
Implementation economics vary between molded, cemented, stitched, and handcrafted footwear.
High style turnover can erode training-data reuse.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-style pilots at domestic factories measuring sustained labor-hour reductions and error rates from AI-assisted tech-pack ingestion, nesting, scheduling, quality inspection, procurement, and documentation through at least two full product cycles.
rho.rationale: Within five years, factories can implement specification parsing, tech-pack checks, scheduling, demand and material planning, customer communication, compliance drafting, and limited machine-vision inspection. Smaller production runs, changing styles, legacy equipment, scarce labeled defect data, physical integration, fit/quality accountability, and customer approval requirements prevent full realization.

s5.bridge: Gallup's fall 2024 survey covers US employer-business owners across industries, not LMM footwear contractors. It reports 22% plan to sell or transfer ownership in five years and 30% among employer owners age 55 or older. I retain 22% as the base and use a range that allows for lower completion and an older manufacturing-owner mix. The proxy can overstate qualifying transfers because plans may not close and may include gifts or internal transfers, while missing unplanned or strategic sales.
caveats: Intentions differ from completed control transfers.
Gallup spans all employer industries and sizes.
A strategic buyer may acquire a brand but close or offshore its factory, failing the transferable-operation test.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession-plan interviews for eligible factories plus five years of closed footwear-factory control transactions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, government-contract novations, industry advisers, and local M&A counsel.
s5.rationale: No industry-specific closed-deal denominator or owner-age distribution was found. The broad employer-owner rate is the least assumptive five-year anchor; domestic footwear's legacy firms may skew older, but that is not used to raise the base without direct evidence.

principal_driver: Repeat per-pair production relationships in specialty niches where domestic speed, compliance, or origin matters.
principal_weakness: A physical production-heavy labor mix sits inside a domestic market with severe import pressure and a poorly measured eligible-firm share.
